title: Listening - Part 2
description: You will hear a woman called Olivia talking about helping with a mobile library project. For questions 9–18, complete the sentences with a word or short phrase.
instructions: You will hear a woman called Olivia talking about helping with a mobile library project. For questions 9–18, complete the sentences with a word or short phrase.

extracts.context: A woman called Olivia talks about helping with a mobile library project.
extracts.audio_script: Hello. I’m Olivia, and I help organise PageTurner, a mobile library service for neighbourhoods on the edge of town. Although people often call it our library bus, we did not begin with a bus at all. In the first year, books were carried around in a second-hand [9]delivery van[/9], which had been donated by a local bakery. It was quite small and shelves had to be removed whenever the vehicle needed repairs. Since then, a larger vehicle has allowed us to carry books more safely and offer activities for readers of different ages.||Our timetable changes slightly each season. We visit schools in the summer term and stop outside a sports centre on Saturdays. During the colder months, the places we visit most frequently are [10]retirement homes[/10], as some older readers find it difficult to travel into the town centre. New volunteers do not drive the vehicle or advise readers immediately. At their introductory session, each person is shown how to use the [11]route map[/11], which lists our stops, arrival times and emergency contact details. It is important that volunteers can explain clearly when the library will return.||Many tasks are simple but useful. Some volunteers return books to shelves, while others help visitors choose large-print titles. Beginners usually start by checking [12]membership forms[/12], making sure names and contact details are complete before cards are prepared. When we stop at summer events, the vehicle can become crowded, so we take out [13]folding tables[/13] and display a selection of books outdoors. These are much easier to transport than ordinary library furniture. A cover is available if there is light rain, although outdoor displays are cancelled in very bad weather.||The type of book people borrow varies more than I expected. Children enjoy adventure stories and adults often ask for travel books, but the category requested most often on our regular route is [14]crime fiction[/14]. We have therefore increased that section. For people who have difficulty reading printed pages, we have also expanded our collection of [15]audio books[/15], which can be borrowed in the same way as ordinary titles. This has been particularly welcomed by people with poor eyesight. We organise seasonal events too. Last year, children collected stickers for every book they completed. This summer we will run a [16]reading challenge[/16], encouraging families to borrow six books during the holiday period.||Volunteering is unpaid, of course, but nobody should lose money by helping us. Anyone working at stops outside their own neighbourhood can claim [17]travel expenses[/17] by keeping bus or train receipts. Finally, we need to replace several damaged children's books. Instead of organising a sponsored walk, we will hold a community [18]book swap[/18] in September. Visitors can exchange books and give a small donation if they wish. All the details are on the PageTurner website, and I have paper application forms for those who would like to volunteer.

dialogue.speaker: olivia
dialogue.text: Hello. I’m Olivia, and I help organise PageTurner, a mobile library service for neighbourhoods on the edge of town. Although people often call it our library bus, we did not begin with a bus at all. In the first year, books were carried around in a second-hand delivery van, which had been donated by a local bakery. It was quite small and shelves had to be removed whenever the vehicle needed repairs. Since then, a larger vehicle has allowed us to carry books more safely and offer activities for readers of different ages.||Our timetable changes slightly each season. We visit schools in the summer term and stop outside a sports centre on Saturdays. During the colder months, the places we visit most frequently are retirement homes, as some older readers find it difficult to travel into the town centre. New volunteers do not drive the vehicle or advise readers immediately. At their introductory session, each person is shown how to use the route map, which lists our stops, arrival times and emergency contact details. Some volunteers return books to shelves, while others help visitors choose large-print titles. Beginners usually start by checking membership forms, making sure names and contact details are complete before cards are prepared. When we stop at summer events, the vehicle can become crowded, so we take out folding tables and display a selection of books outdoors. These are much easier to transport than ordinary library furniture. Children enjoy adventure stories and adults often ask for travel books, but the category requested most often on our regular route is crime fiction. We have therefore increased that section. For people who have difficulty reading printed pages, we have also expanded our collection of audio books, which can be borrowed in the same way as ordinary titles. This has been particularly welcomed by people with poor eyesight. We organise seasonal events too. Last year, children collected stickers for every book they completed. All the details are on the PageTurner website, and I have paper application forms for those who would like to volunteer.

questions.question: The library service originally transported its books in a (9) .
questions.explanation: Olivia says that books were first carried in a second-hand 'delivery van'.

questions.explanation: Olivia explains that the service most frequently visits 'retirement homes' in colder months.

Volunteers working outside their area can receive (17) .
